name: Rifugio Bolzano
description: Historic alpine refuge located in the heart of the Catinaccio group, offering stunning views of the Rosengarten massif. Traditional South Tyrolean hospitality with home-cooked meals and comfortable accommodation.
access_info: Accessible from Carezza Lake via trail 541 (2.5 hours) or from Tires via trail 584 (3 hours). Well-marked paths suitable for experienced hikers.

transport.car: From Carezza Lake (SS241), park at Passo di Costalunga car park (free, 100 spaces). Trailhead at car park.
transport.bus: SAD bus line 180 from Bolzano to Carezza Lake (summer only, twice daily). Stop: Carezza al Lago.

josephine_note: Ask for the Schlutzkrapfen — the cook makes them from scratch every morning. Eat at the terrace table on the east side for the best view of the Rosengarten at sunset.
highlights: Sunset on the Rosengarten massif — turns deep red at dusk
Traditional South Tyrolean half-board dinner included
120-year history as a mountaineers' refuge
booking_note: Book 3–4 weeks ahead in July and August. Phone or email; they prefer phone. Cash only.

name: Rifugio Plose
description: Modern mountain hut on Plose summit with panoramic 360° views of the Dolomites. Accessible by cable car for families and hikers of all levels. Known for exceptional cuisine and sunset dinners.
access_info: Cable car from Bressanone/Brixen (20 minutes) or hiking from Sant'Andrea (4 hours). Multiple trails available for various skill levels.

transport.car: Drive Val Gardena road (SS242) to Selva, then follow signs to Passo Gardena. Park at Passo Gardena (free, 50 spaces).
transport.bus: SAD bus line 471 (Val Gardena express) to Selva di Val Gardena, then taxi or hike 2.5h on trail 666.

josephine_note: The wood-panelled dining room feels like stepping back into 1920s alpine Austria. Order the Knödel soup — it arrives in a cast-iron pot big enough for two.
highlights: Stunning Sella Group massif views from the terrace
Original 1920s wood-panelled alpine interior
Crossroads of Dolomites classic routes — hiker traffic passes all day
booking_note: Essential to book in July–August — fills 4–6 weeks ahead. Online booking at their website.

name: Malga Fane
description: Authentic alpine dairy farm (malga) in a picturesque mountain village. Famous for traditional South Tyrolean cuisine and homemade dairy products. Day visits only - no overnight accommodation.
access_info: Easy 1-hour walk from Valles/Vals parking area. Family-friendly trail through forests and meadows.

transport.car: Drive Passeier valley road (SS44) north from Merano to St. Leonhard in Passeier. Follow signs for Pfelders/Plan. Park at Pfelders village end (free).
transport.bus: SAD bus line 211 from Merano to St. Leonhard, then taxi to Pfelders (no bus to Pfelders). Taxi: +39 0473 656156.

josephine_note: Ring the bell at the gate — the Hofer family has run this malga for four generations. They'll bring out fresh Graukäse and Schüttelbrot before you even sit down. The dairy operates May–October.
highlights: Fresh Graukäse (grey cheese) made on-site daily
Four-generation family farm — authentic Passeier alpine tradition
Wildflower meadow at 1,850m — outstanding in late June
booking_note: Walk-in only; no overnight accommodation. Open daily 9am–5pm in season.

name: Bivacco Latemar
description: Emergency shelter (bivouac) in the Latemar range. Unmanned, always open. Basic accommodation for alpinists and experienced hikers. Bring your own food, sleeping bag, and emergency supplies.
access_info: Challenging alpine route from Passo Costalunga via ferrata sections (5 hours). Experienced hikers with via ferrata equipment only.

transport.car: Park at Lago di Braies (€8/day in season). Hike trail 1 for 3.5h to the bivacco. No road access.
transport.bus: SAD bus line 442 to Lago di Braies (summer only). Stop: Lago di Braies. Then 3.5h hike.

josephine_note: Arrive before sunset — the view west over the Dolomite needles from the door is unlike anything else. Bring your own sleeping bag liner; the blankets are basic. Emergency bivouac: always leave a note in the logbook.
highlights: 360° Dolomite summit panorama — one of the finest in the range
Stargazing from 2,400m with zero light pollution
Logbook with entries going back to 1952 — fascinating reading
booking_note: No booking — first come first served, 8 emergency places. Self-catering only; bring food and a sleeping bag liner.

name: Rifugio Lagazuoi
description: Historic WWI refuge with museum, offering breathtaking views of the Dolomites. Accessible by cable car or challenging hike. Experience alpine history while enjoying modern comforts and gourmet cuisine.
access_info: Lagazuoi cable car from Passo Falzarego (10 minutes) or via ferrata routes for experienced climbers (3-4 hours).

transport.car: From Merano take SS38 north, exit at Lagundo, then road to Tirolo. Park at Tirolo village (free, 10-min walk to cable car). Or drive to Hochmuth via forestry road (4WD recommended).
transport.bus: SAD bus line 206 from Merano to Tirolo (every 30 min). Stop: Tirolo Paese. Then 2h hike on trail 27.

josephine_note: The Almrösti here is the real deal — crispy on the outside, creamy inside, served with house-smoked speck. If you're lucky, the host will play accordion in the evening.
highlights: Classic Tirolean Almrösti with house-smoked Speck
Evening accordion sessions in the original 1958 dining room
Sunrise over Merano city and the Adige valley 800m below
booking_note: Book 1–2 weeks ahead in July–August. Phone preferred: listed in contact.

name: Rifugio Fedare
description: Family-run rifugio since 1932, situated on the famous Tre Cime hiking circuit with panoramic views of the Sexten Dolomites. The hut maintains rustic charm while offering modern comforts, and is celebrated for hearty Ladin mountain cooking and warm hospitality.
access_info: Located on CAI trail 105 connecting Passo Tre Croci and Passo Ghedina (3-4 hours from either pass). Part of the legendary Tre Cime circuit. Well-marked and moderately challenging trail.

transport.car: Parking at Passo Tre Croci (1,809m) or nearby trailheads
transport.bus: SAD bus line 340 to Misurina village, then hiking access via Tre Croci pass

josephine_note: The proprietor's grandmother's recipe for Ladin knödel is still used—they're light as air. Request a table on the north terrace for unobstructed Tre Cime views at sunset. The hut cat, Stella, will adopt you by day two.
highlights: Perfect position on Tre Cime hiking circuit
Authentic Ladin mountain cuisine
Warm family-run atmosphere since 1932
booking_note: Book 4-6 weeks ahead for July-August. Phone or email. 30% deposit required. Accepts bank transfer, card, or PayPal. Flexible cancellation policy.

name: Malga Frara
description: Historic alpine dairy perched above the Passiria Valley since 1756, producing traditional butter and cheese using methods passed down through generations. The remote location and pastoral setting offer visitors an authentic glimpse into Tyrolean mountain life and heritage.
access_info: Accessed via SAT trail 571 from Merano area (2.5 hours) through scenic valley trails. Can also be reached from Passeier Valley trailheads. Moderate difficulty with consistent elevation gain.

transport.car: Road to Passeier Valley villages, then hiking trail start
transport.bus: SAD bus line 240 to Merano, then local bus 241 to Passeier Valley villages

josephine_note: The butter made here is exported to top restaurants in Bolzano. Stay two nights and help with the afternoon milking—it's therapeutic. The yodeling at dinner on Saturdays is authentic, not for tourists.
highlights: Operating since 1756 with unchanged methods
Pristine Passiria Valley location
Authentic pastoral experience
booking_note: Call ahead, this is a working farm. Cash only. Families and groups preferred. Advance notice required for dinner reservations.

name: Rifugio Nuvolau
description: Historic rifugio at the summit of Ra Gusela with 360-degree views of the Cortina amphitheater and surrounding peaks. Operating since 1898, it's one of the most iconic mountain huts in the Dolomites, offering luxury alpine hospitality and Michelin-quality cuisine.
access_info: Cable car from Cortina (10 minutes) or CAI trail 213 from town (1.5 hours). Hut is located directly on the famous Cinque Torri circuit trail CAI 209.

transport.car: Cable car parking in Cortina center
transport.bus: SAD bus line 423 directly to cable car station in Cortina

josephine_note: Book room 28 for sunrise over the Tre Cime—it's reserved for returning guests. The pappardelle ai funghi porcini is extraordinary in September. The wine list includes 400+ selections; ask Pietro for recommendations.
highlights: Icon of Italian alpine hospitality
360-degree Cortina amphitheater views
Michelin-quality mountain cuisine
booking_note: Book 8-12 weeks ahead for summer. Website booking system or phone. Credit card required to hold. 50% deposit due at booking, balance 1 week prior. Premium cancellation policy.

name: Rifugio Elmo
description: Welcoming rifugio on the slopes of Monte Elmo with panoramic views of the Vinschgau Valley and Ortler massif. Family-owned since 1954, it serves as a base camp for hikers exploring the scenic trails of the Venoste Valley with hearty regional cuisine and reliable mountain hospitality.
access_info: Located on SAT trail 2C connecting Merano and Naturns (2 hours from either town). Also accessible via SAT 11 from Algund village (1.5 hours). Well-maintained paths suitable for families and casual hikers.

transport.car: Road access to Algund village, then hiking trail or shuttle service
transport.bus: SAD bus line 240 to Merano, then line 241 to Algund village for trail access

josephine_note: The terrace drinks at sunset with Ortler views are unbeatable. Try the Vinschgau apple strudel—it's grandmother's recipe. The owner's son is a mountain guide; ask about private guided walks.
highlights: Ortler massif and valley views
Excellent family-run hospitality
Perfect base for Vinschgau exploration
booking_note: Book 3-4 weeks ahead. Email or phone. 25% deposit required. Accepts bank transfer and card. Flexible cancellation with 1 week notice.

name: Bivacco Ghedina
description: Emergency shelter and bivacco operated by CAI Bolzano on Passo Ghedina, providing basic protection for climbers and hikers in unexpected weather or emergencies. Unmanned facility with emergency supplies and contact information posted inside.
access_info: Located directly on Passo Ghedina (2,533m) along SAT trail 559. Accessible from Val Gardena side (45 minutes from Selva) or from Cortina side via CAI 211 (1.5 hours).

transport.car: Road access to Passo Ghedina parking area
transport.bus: SAD bus line 341 to Passo Ghedina pass area

josephine_note: Emergency use only, but well-stocked with CAI information about surrounding trails. There's a guest book—leaving a note has become tradition for ambitious hikers.
highlights: Free emergency shelter
Year-round access
Strategic alpine pass location
booking_note: No booking needed. Emergency shelter—open to all. No services. Maintained by CAI Bolzano volunteers.

name: Rifugio Tre Croci
description: Family-friendly rifugio at the famous Tre Croci pass, a major junction for hikers and cyclists exploring the Dolomites. Operating since 1925, the hut welcomes all mountain enthusiasts with simple comfort and traditional mountain food in a convivial atmosphere.
access_info: Located directly on Passo Tre Croci at the intersection of CAI trails 104, 105, and 107. Gateway to Tre Cime circuit and Sexten Dolomites. Multiple trail options from Misurina (1.5 hours), Auronzo (2 hours), and surrounding valleys.

transport.car: Road access to Passo Tre Croci with parking
transport.bus: SAD bus line 340 to Misurina village, then hiking access via pass (45 minutes)

josephine_note: The owner keeps detailed trail condition information—ask about current conditions before early/late season hikes. The apple juice is fresh-pressed from local orchards. Stay overnight and watch sunrise over the Tre Cime from the eastern terrace.
highlights: Gateway to Tre Cime circuit
Historic pass location with multiple trail junctions
Family-friendly mountain hospitality
booking_note: Book 2-3 weeks ahead. Phone or email. 25% deposit required. Accepts cash and card. Flexible cancellation up to 1 week before arrival.

name: Rifugio Saslong
description: Prestigious rifugio positioned on the Sasso Lungo (Sassolungo) massif, accessible via the famous Saslong ski descent route. Modern comfort with traditional charm, offering exceptional views of the Val Gardena and surrounding Dolomite peaks. Known for refined alpine cuisine and professional service.
access_info: Accessible via cable car from Selva Val Gardena (20 minutes) or hiking trail SAT 557 (2 hours from village). Can also be reached from Passo Sella via trail 558 (1.5 hours). Multiple access options for varying abilities.

transport.car: Cable car parking in Selva Val Gardena village center
transport.bus: SAD bus line 341 directly to Selva Val Gardena cable car station

josephine_note: The chef studied at a Michelin restaurant in Valle d'Aosta—it shows in every plate. Room 24 has a private balcony facing Sassolungo at sunset. The white wine from nearby vineyards is exceptional with the seafood pasta.
highlights: Iconic Sassolungo massif location
Modern comfort with mountain elegance
Refined alpine cuisine
booking_note: Book 5-7 weeks ahead for July-August. Website or email. 35% deposit required. Accepts card, bank transfer, or PayPal. Standard cancellation policy with 2 weeks notice.

name: Bivacco Passo Ghedina
description: An unmanned emergency bivacco managed by CAI, offering basic shelter in a stone hut with wooden bunks and a small stove. Located on the high Passo Ghedina between Ortler and Cevedale peaks, it serves climbers and mountaineers as a safe refuge point. No amenities, supplies, or services; suitable for experienced mountaineers only.
access_info: Accessible via CAI trail 2/A from Passo dello Stelvio (2758 m), approximately 2.5 hours descending to the pass. Alternatively via CAI trail 3 from Val Venosta (4+ hours of steep ascent). Alpine route; exposure and scrambling involved; not for casual hikers.

transport.car: Passo dello Stelvio car park (€3/day summer) is the nearest motorized access point, 450 m higher. SP40 is a famous switchback alpine road, closed November–May.
transport.bus: No direct bus service. Nearest SAD bus stop is Gomagoi (Val Venosta); taxi or private transport required for Passo dello Stelvio access.

josephine_note: This is an emergency shelter, not a hut. Bring your own sleeping bag, food, water, and stove fuel. Winter conditions can be extreme; snow buildup may block entry. Always notify someone of your itinerary and expected return.
highlights: Free emergency shelter for mountaineers
Key waypoint on alpine traverse routes
Year-round access for experienced climbers
booking_note: No booking possible; first-come basis. Free to use. Maintain the hut—pack out all waste. Report significant damage to CAI Merano section. Register in logbook.

name: Rifugio Auronzo
description: Built in 1910 on the eastern flank of the Tre Cime di Lavaredo, Rifugio Auronzo offers breathtaking views of the iconic dolomitic peaks. This historic hut is a legendary waypoint on the Tre Cime circuit and features traditional Ladin hospitality. The rifugio has been family-run for four generations and remains one of the most visited alpine huts in the Dolomites.
access_info: Accessible via CAI trail 101 from Auronzo di Cadore (2 hours). Also reachable via the famous Tre Cime circuit (trail 101/103). A steep cable car (Funivia Auronzo) operates seasonally for those preferring easier access.

transport.car: Parking available at Passo Tre Croci (1809m); cable car station 200m walk
transport.bus: ATAP bus service to Auronzo di Cadore; nearest stop Auronzo Centro (15km from trailhead)

josephine_note: Order the casunziei (half-moon pasta pockets with spinach and ricotta) for dinner—they're made fresh daily by the owner's mother. Request a room on the south-facing side for sunrise views that will change your life. The hut's terrace at golden hour is unmissable.
highlights: Unobstructed views of Tre Cime di Lavaredo
Historic cable car option for less experienced hikers
Authentic Ladin cuisine and family hospitality
booking_note: Book 2–3 weeks ahead in July–August. Accepts online reservations via website or WhatsApp. 50% deposit required; balance payable on arrival (cash or card accepted).

name: Malga Zannes
description: A working alpine dairy farm since 1925, Malga Zannes produces its own cheese and butter from the milk of cows grazing the high pastures of Val Gardena. The malga maintains traditional cheesemaking practices and offers visitors a glimpse into authentic mountain farming life. Guests can watch the afternoon milking and enjoy freshly made ricotta in the hut's simple dining room.
access_info: Reached via SAT trail 11 from Ortisei village center (90 minutes) or trail 13 from Passo Ghedina (1.5 hours). Well-marked, moderate grade with some steep sections. Spectacular meadow approach in June–July during wild flower bloom.

transport.car: No vehicle access; nearest parking at Ortisei town center (1.5 km walk to trailhead)
transport.bus: SAD line 342 serves Ortisei; hut 1.5 km uphill from bus stop

josephine_note: Arrive by 3pm to watch the evening milking—it's a genuine working operation, not a tourist show. The fresh ricotta warm from the morning batch (available only in early summer) is extraordinary; ask Maria if you can have it with honey. Sleep in the lower barn rooms if you want authentic silence and the sounds of cattle.
highlights: Active cheese and butter production on-site
Genuine alpine dairy farm experience
Wildflower meadows and quiet mountain setting
booking_note: Book 10–14 days ahead, especially for weekends. Accepts phone or WhatsApp reservations; email confirmation. Cash preferred; card accepted with advance notice.

name: Rifugio Tre Scarperi
description: Named after the three rocky peaks ('scarperi' in local dialect) that dominate the skyline, Rifugio Tre Scarperi sits at the junction of Val Pusteria and the remote Tre Croci plateau. Rebuilt in 2010 with modern comfort while preserving traditional architecture, the hut is known for its warm Pusterian welcome and innovative mountain cuisine. The rifugio attracts hikers, mountaineers, and nature photographers seeking solitude without sacrificing amenities.
access_info: Accessible via CAI trail 6 from Dobbiaco (2.5 hours) or CAI trail 7 from Cortina d'Ampezzo (3 hours). The plateau approach from Tre Croci is gentler but longer. Excellent conditions most of the year; winter approach requires mountaineering experience.

transport.car: Parking at Tre Croci pass (2054m); 3.5 km trail walk to hut
transport.bus: Dolomiti superski shuttle bus to Tre Croci pass from Dobbiaco (20 minutes); nearest town buses stop in Dobbiaco

nearby_trails: CAI 6 - Val Pusteria to Tre Scarperi
josephine_note: The kitchen here experiments with 'modern mountain' dishes—try the venison ragu with tagliatelle if it's on the menu. Sunrise from the west-facing balcony is dramatic; set your alarm. The owner, Klaus, is a wildlife photographer; ask him about the marmot colony near the hut and he might let you borrow binoculars.
highlights: Modern comfort in a remote alpine setting
Innovative Pusterian cuisine with local ingredients
Gateway to the isolated Tre Croci plateau
booking_note: Book 2–3 weeks ahead for peak season (July–September). Online booking via website or email. 40% non-refundable deposit; remainder due on arrival. Accepts cash and card.

name: Bivacco Zöscheles
description: A basic emergency shelter on the high alpine ridge between Merano and the Zillertal, Bivacco Zöscheles offers refuge for mountaineers and fastpackers on the SAT high-route traverse. Built in 1987 and maintained by the SAT (Società degli Alpinisti Trentini), this tiny stone structure provides emergency bunks, a small stove, and water from a nearby spring. No amenities, no advance reservations—this is pure alpine minimalism for self-sufficient travelers.
access_info: Reached only via SAT high-route trail 1/2 from Passeier Joch (2.5 hours) or Merano area (6–7 hours). Exposed ridge approach; helmet recommended in exposed sections. Route-finding requires map and compass; not suitable for beginners.

transport.car: No vehicle access. Nearest parking at Merano town (30 km away); several days required to approach on foot
transport.bus: No direct access. SAD buses serve Merano; approach requires full trekking approach from valley

josephine_note: This is not a vacation hut—it's a lifeline for mountain traversers. Bring a sleeping bag, water bottle, and emergency food. The logbook has entries from mountaineers doing multi-day ridge walks; read it before sleeping to understand who passed through. Water from the spring below the shelter is reliable even in late summer.
highlights: Emergency shelter on the high alpine traverse
Free, open year-round access
Exposed ridge position with dramatic views
booking_note: No booking system. First-come, first-served. Free. Bring all supplies. Assume other mountaineers may be present; respect shared space.

name: Rifugio Payer
description: Situated on the high plateau between the Ortler and Cevedale massifs, Rifugio Payer (named after the pioneering mountaineer Julius Payer) is a hub for alpine mountaineers and serious hikers tackling the 3000m+ peaks of the Ortler Alps. The hut underwent a major renovation in 2015 and now combines historical significance with modern alpine hospitality. Its position at the gateway to some of South Tyrol's highest summits makes it legendary among climbers.
access_info: Accessible via CAI trail 12 from Sulden/Solda (2.5 hours) or trail 14 from the Stelvio Pass road (2 hours walking). The hut serves as a base camp for Ortler (3905m) and Cevedale (3769m) ascents. Well-maintained paths; exposure and altitude require acclimatization.

transport.car: Parking at Solda village (2010m); cable car (Seilbahn Solda) operates summer months to 2600m (500m walk remaining)
transport.bus: STSA bus line 260 from Merano to Solda village (90 minutes); nearest stop Solda town center

josephine_note: If you're climbing Ortler the next day, eat a light dinner (polenta, not a heavy meat dish) and ask the hut to prepare a thermos of espresso and a packed breakfast for 4am. The south wall views at sunrise are hypnotic. The hut's naturalist guide, Francesco, runs expert bird-watching walks in June; worth booking ahead.
highlights: Gateway to Ortler and Cevedale peaks
Historic mountaineering hut with modern amenities
Stunning panoramic views of the Ortler Alps
booking_note: Book 3–4 weeks ahead for July–August, especially if planning a summit day. Online reservations via website. For climbers planning peak attempts, contact by phone to arrange early breakfast/packed lunch. 50% deposit required; balance on arrival.

name: Malga Fedare
description: Family-run alpine dairy farm producing fresh Ladin cheese and ricotta, nestled in the heart of Val Gardena pastureland. Malga Fedare maintains traditional cheesemaking practices dating to 1968 and offers rustic accommodation in a converted barn. Simple, authentic meals featuring their own dairy products define the experience here.
access_info: Reached via CAI trail 3 from Ortisei village (1.5 hours uphill on well-maintained path). Winter access by snowshoe; summer suitable for families and moderate hikers. The final approach crosses open meadow.

transport.car: Drive to Ortisei town center; parking at Piazza Mehrdau, then hike trailhead at Strada Col Raiser
transport.bus: SAD Line 338 (Ortisei–Selva, stop Ortisei Ciasa Gherdeina, 200m from trailhead)

josephine_note: Arrive by 4pm to watch the evening milking and cheese-pressing ritual—you can help if interested. The fresh ricotta served warm at breakfast is life-changing; don't skip it. Bring cash only; they operate entirely offline and prefer it that way.
highlights: Working dairy farm with visitor access to traditional cheesemaking
Authentic Ladin culture and dialect preserved in a family setting
No electricity noise—pure alpine quiet and dark skies at night
booking_note: Book 1–2 weeks ahead via email or WhatsApp; phone calls preferred. Cash deposit equal to one night required to secure reservation. Minimum 2-night stay encouraged.

name: Rifugio Tre Scarperi
description: Modern rifugio with traditional Tyrolean architecture, perched on a sunny plateau overlooking Val Pusteria and the Sexten Dolomites. Renovated in 2019, Tre Scarperi combines contemporary comfort with local stone and wood construction. The hut is a preferred base for trail runners and mountain bikers exploring the high valleys.
access_info: CAI trail 31A from Passo Tre Croci parking (1 hour ascent, moderate gradient) or trail 33 from Dürnstein village (2 hours). Mountain bikes permitted on designated paths; horses welcome.

transport.car: Mountain road to Passo Tre Croci car park (1,800m); shuttle minibus from park to hut parking (3km, €5 per person in summer)
transport.bus: SAD Line 432 to Dürnstein village (30 min walk to trailhead via trail 33)

josephine_note: The hut dog (a St. Bernard named Marco) adopts hikers for the duration of their stay—he's famous on social media. Skip the standard menu and ask the chef about the daily special; it's always outstanding and locally sourced. Book Room 12 for the sunrise view over Tre Cime.
highlights: Sunrise views across the entire Sexten Dolomite range and Val Pusteria below
Full-service hut with spa shower and sauna available (€8 supplement)
Gateway to excellent trail-running circuits and mountain-bike singletrack
booking_note: Advance booking recommended 2–3 weeks for weekends; walk-ins accepted mid-week. Online booking or phone; €25 deposit per person. Free cancellation up to 10 days prior.

name: Rifugio Kassianerhütte
description: Intimate Tyrolean hut on the slopes above Merano, managed by the same family for three generations. Kassianerhütte is beloved for its Apfel Strudel (apple cake) and wine list featuring local Merano producers. The quiet setting appeals to hikers seeking refuge from crowded valley trails.
access_info: CAI trail 24 from Merano-Obermais district (1.5 hours moderate ascent through chestnut and larch forest). Well-marked path suitable for families; no technical difficulty. Alternative descent via trail 24B adds 30 minutes but offers extended views.

transport.car: Park in Merano town (Parkhaus Europa); public transit or taxi to Obermais trailhead (5min by car)
transport.bus: SAD Line 1A to Obermais stop; trailhead 200m south at Kirchplatz

josephine_note: The strudel is baked fresh daily at 3pm; ask if you can watch through the kitchen window. Grandmother Margot (age 87) still works the dining room on weekends and loves sharing stories in German and Italian. The south-facing terrace at sunset is perfect for a glass of Vermentino from the Val d'Adige.
highlights: Three-generation family management with authentic Tyrolean hospitality
Easy access from Merano town, yet feels remote and quiet
House-made pastries and curated regional wine selection
booking_note: Book 1–2 weeks ahead via email or WhatsApp; small hut, fills quickly in weekends. €20 deposit per room. Cancellation accepted up to 14 days prior.

name: Bivacco Marzelini
description: Emergency shelter bivacco on the high ridge of Marzelini above Vinschgau, maintained by CAI Naturno section. Bare-bones metal refuge with wooden bunks and mattresses; no supplies or utilities beyond weather protection. Intended for mountaineers and trekkers caught in unexpected weather or benighted on alpine crossings.
access_info: Reached via CAI trail 18 from Karthaus (3.5 hours scramble, exposed sections in descent). Trail 18A variant from Passo del Gallo adds 1 hour but avoids difficult terrain. Exposed ridge-walking; not suitable for inexperienced mountaineers.

transport.car: Park at Karthaus village, Parkplatz Quellweg (paid lot, €2/day). No vehicular access beyond village.
transport.bus: SAD Line 220 to Karthaus church (50m from Parkplatz); 4 daily connections to Merano

josephine_note: Bring your own everything—water, food, sleeping mat, and warm clothes. The logbook inside documents decades of mountaineering stories; read entries from the 1980s for a laugh. Winter access requires crampons and rope knowledge; only experts attempt it after November.
highlights: Free emergency shelter open year-round in remote alpine setting
Historic CAI refuge with rustic charm and mountaineering heritage
Gateway to high-altitude ridge traverses and scrambling adventures in Vinschgau
booking_note: No booking necessary; first-come, first-served basis. Free; donations to CAI Naturno accepted. Bring headtorch, map, compass, and self-sufficiency.

name: Rifugio Gomagoi
description: Remote and historic rifugio nestled in the Gruppo di Tessa massif above the wine valleys of Merano, offering a dramatic escape from the tourist-filled valley floor. Built in 1948 and recently updated, the hut retains its pioneering spirit while providing solid alpine hospitality and access to rarely-crowded trails. An insider's choice for those seeking solitude and authentic mountain experience.
access_info: Accessed via SAT trail 27 from Passo del Giogo (2178m) in approximately 1.5 hours, or longer approach via SAT trail 28 from Hafling/Avelengo (3.5 hours, more scenic). The final 300m gain is steep but well-maintained with some fixed cables. Trail is red-marked and moderately challenging.

transport.car: Parking at Passo del Giogo; mountain road from Merano via Hafling (unpaved section, passable May–October)
transport.bus: SAD bus line 4 from Merano to Hafling village; then hiking (no direct bus to Passo del Giogo in summer)

nearby_trails: SAT 26 to Rifugio Gampen (4 hours, high alpine traverse)
josephine_note: Thomas, the keeper, makes exceptional ploetzky (small fried pastries with sauerkraut and speck) for breakfast—ask for extra. The hut's north-facing location means cool nights perfect for sleeping and minimal crowds; it's refreshingly quiet even in August. Bring a headlamp for the final trail section at dusk; it's steeper than it looks on the map.
highlights: Secluded high-altitude location rarely visited by tour groups, maintaining authentic alpine atmosphere
Strategic position for multi-day traverses across the Tessa massif and toward Rifugio Gampen
Family-run with personality; small intimate hut (40 beds) feels like staying with mountain friends
booking_note: Book 3–4 weeks ahead June–September. Email or phone; payment by bank transfer (10% deposit) or cash on arrival. Cancellation free up to 10 days before. Groups of 8+ require advance notice.

name: Bivacco Sassolungo
description: Emergency bivacco (4-person capacity with foam mats) maintained by CAI Sarnthal section on the remote northern flank of Sassolungo. Spartan but weatherproof shelter offering protection for mountaineers engaged in the challenging Sassolungo traverse. No amenities or services; purely a life-saving waypoint for those caught out or acclimatizing.
access_info: Accessible only via CAI trail 5/6 from Val Sarentino (extremely steep and exposed, 4+ hours from Passo Sarner, 2637m). Scramble involves grade I–II climbing on secure but airy terrain. Not recommended for casual hikers; mountaineering experience essential. Alpine clubs maintain this shelter for emergencies only.

transport.car: Parking at Passo Sarner; rough 4WD road from Sarentino village (summer only)
transport.bus: SAD bus line 450 from Bolzano to Sarentino village (daily, ~1.5 hours); then private transfer or hiking required to Passo Sarner

josephine_note: This is NOT a romantic alpine getaway; it's a lifeline. The bivacco is unheated, has no water (melt snow in summer), and smells like decades of mountaineers. Bring your own food, stove, and fuel. The logbook is humbling—read entries from climbers who relied on this shelter during unexpected storms. Respect the mountains.
highlights: Emergency shelter for serious mountaineers on exposed high-altitude terrain
Free, year-round access—maintained by CAI for safety, not profit
Extreme alpine experience: bare minimum amenities, maximum mountain reality
booking_note: No booking needed or possible. Free, emergency access. Bring all supplies. Register with CAI Sarnthal if possible for safety tracking. This is self-rescue territory; only experienced alpinists should attempt this route.

name: Rifugio Auronzo
description: Historic rifugio built in 1952 on the iconic Three Peaks circuit, offering panoramic views of the Tre Cime di Lavaredo. Family-run establishment known for its warm hospitality and traditional Dolomite cuisine featuring casunziei and speck.
access_info: Accessible via CAI trail 101 from Auronzo di Cadore (2.5 hours) or SAT trail 105 from the Tre Cime parking area (1.5 hours). Well-maintained paths suitable for experienced hikers.

transport.car: Parking available at Tre Cime di Lavaredo (30 min walk uphill) or at Auronzo di Cadore base station
transport.bus: SAD bus line 6 from Cortina d'Ampezzo to Auronzo di Cadore; no direct bus to hut

nearby_trails: SAI 110 - Tre Cime to Rifugio Sorau
josephine_note: Order the casunziei con formaggio e patate (cheese and potato pockets) — made fresh daily by Marisa, the owner's daughter. Sit on the western terrace at sunset for views that'll make you weep. The hut occasionally closes mid-week in shoulder season; call ahead.
highlights: Direct views of Tre Cime di Lavaredo from the terrace
Legendary casunziei prepared daily with local ingredients
Central hub for the most famous Dolomite hiking circuit
booking_note: Book 2–3 weeks ahead in July–August. Email preferred. 50% deposit required via bank transfer; balance payable in cash at arrival.

name: Rifugio Locatelli
description: Intimate rifugio named after the legendary alpinist Riccardo Locatelli, perched on a rocky outcrop between Tre Cime and Sorau. Rustic charm with minimal amenities but exceptional mountain cuisine and breathtaking 360° views.
access_info: Accessible via CAI trail 103 from the Tre Cime parking area (2 hours) or via SAT trail 109 from Rifugio Sorau (1.5 hours). Exposed sections with fixed cables in places; suitable for experienced hikers only.

transport.car: Parking at Tre Cime di Lavaredo main lot; then hiking trail ascent. No road access to hut.
transport.bus: No direct bus; nearest is SAD line 6 to Tre Cime parking area (requires hiking up)

nearby_trails: CAI 103 - Tre Cime to Rifugio Locatelli
josephine_note: Cash only — plan accordingly. The owner, Giancarlo, makes pasta fresca daily; try his speck and spinach ravioli. The south-facing rooms catch alpenglow on Tre Cime in the evening — absolutely stunning but book the west side. No showers means quick wash-ups at the sink; embrace it.
highlights: Ultra-exposed alpine setting with 360° Dolomite vistas
Handmade fresh pasta prepared daily by owner
Rewarding hike through dramatic rocky terrain
booking_note: Book 2–3 weeks ahead for July–August. Phone call strongly preferred. Cash payment only; no deposits possible.

name: Bivacco Fedare
description: Small emergency mountain shelter managed by CAI Pusteria, providing basic refuge on the high ridge between Tre Cime and Paternsattel. Unmanned, minimalist structure with emergency mats and water supply.
access_info: Accessible via CAI trail 103/104 from Tre Cime area (3 hours) or via CAI 106 from Misurina (4 hours). Exposed alpine ridge; route-finding required; proper equipment and experience essential.

transport.car: No vehicle access. Parking at Tre Cime di Lavaredo or Misurina lake
transport.bus: SAD line 6 to Tre Cime parking area; then 3+ hours hiking

josephine_note: This is a true emergency shelter, not a destination. Bring your own sleeping pad, stove fuel, and water. It's open year-round but only visit if weather deteriorates during a hike; don't plan to stay here for the experience. The emergency water cistern is reliable but taste questionable.
highlights: Free emergency refuge on exposed Dolomite ridge
Year-round access for mountain emergencies
Minimal impact on wild alpine environment
booking_note: No booking needed. Unmanned shelter, open year-round. Free. Respect the space; leave it clean for the next person. Report any damage to CAI Pusteria.

name: Rifugio Hochmuth
description: Classic Tyrolean hut perched on the plateau above Tirolo, with sweeping views over Merano and the Adige Valley. Family-run since 1958, it serves hearty meals and is the natural finish for the Hochmuth Trail — one of South Tyrol's most popular walks.
access_info: Via trail 27 from Tirolo village (1.5 hours) or trail 9A from Merano Obermais (2 hours). Well-marked, moderate ascent. Accessible by mountain bike on the lower section.
josephine_note: Order the Schlutzkrapfen — the cook makes them from scratch and they arrive still steaming. The terrace table facing Merano is the best seat in South Tyrol for a Sunday lunch.
highlights: Panoramic view over Merano and Adige Valley
Traditional Tyrolean Schlutzkrapfen made fresh daily
Natural finish of the famous Hochmuth Trail
booking_note: Book 1–2 weeks ahead on weekends. Phone preferred. Cash and card accepted.

name: Rifugio Taser
description: Quiet refuge on the Taser high plateau above Schenna, surrounded by larch forest and meadows. One of the less-visited huts near Merano — a local favourite for its peaceful setting and generous South Tyrolean portions.
access_info: Trail 4 from Schenna village (1.5 hours) or trail 7 from Hafling (2 hours). Both well-maintained and suitable for families with older children.
josephine_note: The owner keeps a small vegetable garden — whatever is ripe ends up on the menu that evening. Arrive before 4pm on weekdays and you might have the whole terrace to yourself.
highlights: Secluded larch-forest setting
Garden-fresh seasonal menu
Peaceful alternative to the busier Merano huts
booking_note: Walk-ins welcome. Phone ahead only if coming as a group of 5 or more.

name: Rifugio Val di Nova
description: Alpine hut at the entrance of Val di Nova in the Gruppo di Tessa Nature Park, popular with trail runners and trekkers on the Meraner Höhenweg long-distance route. The hut sits at a natural crossroads with access to some of the finest ridge walks above Merano.
access_info: Trail 24 from Merano Obermais (2 hours) or the upper cable car from Merano to Falzeben and trail 23 (45 minutes). Very well marked.
josephine_note: This is a key stop on the Meraner Höhenweg — hikers arrive with real hunger. The apple strudel here is worth the climb alone. Stay overnight and start the ridge walk early before the clouds build.
highlights: Key stop on the Meraner Höhenweg long-distance route
Spectacular Group di Tessa Nature Park setting
Excellent apple strudel made fresh daily
booking_note: Book 2–3 weeks ahead July–August. Online booking or phone. Card and cash accepted.

name: Rifugio Falzeben
description: One of the most accessible huts above Merano, reachable in 20 minutes by cable car from the city. Falzeben sits on a sunny plateau with views south over Merano and north toward the Texelgruppe peaks. A beloved local destination for day trips and multi-day hiking base.
access_info: Cable car from Merano (Seilbahn Meran 2000) to Falzeben station — 20 minutes, runs daily May–October. Or hike trail 34 from Hafling village (1 hour). Family friendly.
josephine_note: Perfect for non-hikers in your group — take the cable car up and they can sit on the sun terrace with a glass of local Vernatsch while you tackle the ridge. The Speckknödel here are legendary.
highlights: Cable car access — perfect for mixed-ability groups
Sun terrace with panoramic Merano valley views
Famous Speckknödel soup
booking_note: Walk-ins welcome for day meals. Book overnight 1–2 weeks ahead in summer.

name: Rifugio Gampen
description: Traditional mountain hut at Gampenpass above Naturns, on the ancient Vinschgau trade route. The hut retains its original stone architecture and offers an atmosphere that feels unchanged since the 1930s. Popular starting point for walks into the Texelgruppe Nature Park.
access_info: Drive Gampenpass road from Naturns (paved, 20 minutes) or hike trail 1 from Naturns village (2.5 hours). Parking available at the pass.
josephine_note: The stone walls of this hut are 90 years old and they keep everything cool — even on August days it feels like a different world. The Vinschgauer bread baked on-site is extraordinary with local butter.
highlights: Original 1930s stone architecture
Gateway to Texelgruppe Nature Park
Authentic historic atmosphere — no Wi-Fi, no noise
booking_note: Walk-ins welcome. Overnight bookings recommended for July–August weekends.

name: Malga Verano
description: Working dairy farm above the village of Verano (Vöran), producing Graukäse and fresh ricotta. The farm family has grazed cattle on this plateau for six generations. Day visitors are welcomed for cheese plates, cold drinks and simple meals at wooden tables outside.
access_info: Easy walk from Verano village car park via trail 42 (45 minutes, flat terrain). Ideal for families and non-hikers.
josephine_note: Ring the bell at the wooden gate — the cheese plate they bring out (Graukäse, Schüttelbrot, local butter) costs almost nothing and is some of the best food in South Tyrol.
highlights: Six-generation family dairy farm
Fresh Graukäse made on-site
Easy walk — accessible for all ages
booking_note: Walk-in only. Open daily 9am–5pm in season.

name: Malga Seefeld
description: Peaceful alpine dairy above Naturns with a small pond (Seefeld — 'field by the lake') reflecting the surrounding peaks. The farm produces butter and fresh cheese June through October, and serves simple traditional meals on its outdoor terrace. A favourite stop on the Meraner Höhenweg circuit.
access_info: Trail 8 from Naturns village (2 hours) or trail 22 from Gampenpass (45 minutes). Well-signed. Part of the Meraner Höhenweg long-distance route.
josephine_note: The small pond in front of the malga reflects the Texel peaks perfectly at midday. Sit down with a Buttermilch and fresh Schüttelbrot and let yourself stop — properly stop — for an hour.
highlights: Reflective mountain pond setting
On the Meraner Höhenweg route
Fresh buttermilk and house-made cheese boards
booking_note: Walk-in only. Open daily when cows are on the high pasture — typically 9am–4pm.

name: Malga Kirchsteiger
description: Small family malga on the southern slopes above Schenna, producing traditional Schüttelbrot (cracker bread) alongside fresh dairy products. One of the few malghe where visitors can watch the bread-baking process — the stone oven has been in continuous use since 1894.
access_info: Trail 6 from Schenna village (1 hour, gentle ascent through apple orchards). Suitable for all fitness levels.
josephine_note: Visit on a Tuesday or Friday — those are the baking days. The smell of Schüttelbrot coming from the stone oven is unlike anything else. Buy a bag to take home.
highlights: Stone bread oven in use since 1894
Watch traditional Schüttelbrot being baked
Gentle walk through apple orchards from Schenna
booking_note: Walk-in only. Baking days: Tuesday and Friday. Cheese and bread available daily when open.

name: Malga Leiter
description: High-altitude malga at the edge of the Texelgruppe Nature Park, reached by a scenic ridge trail above Merano. The Leiter family has managed the pasture since 1912. The altitude means this is one of the last malghe to open and first to close — but the cheese is exceptional and the views extraordinary.
access_info: Trail 34 from Hafling cable car station (1.5 hours, moderate with some steep sections) or trail 35 from Merano 2000 ski area (2 hours). Not suitable for young children.
josephine_note: The short season makes this special. Come in July when the wildflowers are at peak. The aged Almkäse (mountain cheese) here is sold by the wheel — buy a piece and it'll last weeks.
highlights: Short season makes it rare and special
Edge of Texelgruppe Nature Park
Exceptional aged mountain cheese
booking_note: Walk-in only. Usually open 9am–3pm when weather permits.

name: Malga Pfelderer
description: Classic Passeier Valley malga at the end of the Pfelders valley road, a traditional endpoint for families from Merano making a Sunday drive. The farm dairy opens a simple kitchen in summer serving cold plates, soups and house-pressed apple juice alongside the cheese products.
access_info: Drive Passeiertal (SS44) north from Merano to St. Leonhard, then follow signs to Pfelders (15 km, paved). Park at the village end and walk 20 minutes to the malga. Or hike trail 211 from St. Leonhard (2.5 hours).
josephine_note: On Sunday mornings the whole valley comes here — it's a ritual. Arrive before 10am for the freshest ricotta. The house-pressed apple juice is from their own orchard below.
highlights: Classic Passeier Valley Sunday destination
House-pressed apple juice from own orchard
Easy access by car — ideal for families
booking_note: Walk-in only. Busy on Sunday mornings — arrive early.

name: Bivacco Talbauer
description: Small emergency shelter maintained by CAI Merano above the Talbauer plateau, on the high route connecting the Texelgruppe peaks. 8 emergency bunks with foam mats. No utilities — bring all supplies. Year-round access for experienced mountaineers.
access_info: Trail 35 from Hafling/Avelengo (3 hours, exposed sections above 1900m) or trail 36 from Merano 2000 ski station (2 hours). Route-finding required in poor visibility.
josephine_note: Useful emergency waypoint on the Texelgruppe traverse. Bring your own sleeping bag, stove and food. The spring 100m below the shelter is reliable in summer; check the logbook for current conditions.
highlights: Emergency shelter on Texelgruppe high route
Year-round access
Reliable water source nearby in summer
booking_note: No booking — free, first-come. Sign the logbook on arrival. Bring all supplies.

name: Bivacco Riffler
description: Exposed emergency bivouac below the Riffler summit ridge in the Texelgruppe massif. 6 emergency places with no amenities. Reserved for alpinists engaged in technical routes on the Texel peaks. Not accessible to casual hikers.
access_info: Alpine route only — from Malga Lazins via trail 37B (4 hours, grade II scrambling, some fixed cables). Helmet and harness recommended. Technical alpinism experience required.
josephine_note: This is serious alpine terrain. Check conditions with CAI Merano before attempting. In good conditions the sunrise from the ridge above the bivouac is extraordinary. Always leave a route plan with someone.
highlights: High-altitude alpine shelter in the Texelgruppe
Gateway to technical Texel peak routes
Free and year-round for experienced alpinists
booking_note: Emergency use only. No booking possible. Register your route with CAI Merano for safety.

name: Bivacco Schneekoppe
description: Tiny 4-person shelter on the ridge above Val di Nova, built in 1971 as an emergency refuge for hikers caught by afternoon storms on the exposed Gruppe di Tessa trails. Maintained by local alpine volunteers. Small but well-constructed, with a small logbook and emergency whistle posted inside.
access_info: From Rifugio Val di Nova via trail 23B (1 hour from hut, exposed ridge walking). Only accessible in good weather — the ridge is dangerous in storms or poor visibility. Map and compass required.
josephine_note: This shelter was built after a fatal accident in 1969. Read the plaque inside when you arrive. The views on a clear day are extraordinary — Merano below, Ortler to the west, Texel peaks north.
highlights: Historic 1971 emergency shelter with a story
Ridge views: Merano, Ortler, Texel peaks
Small and intimate — 4 places only
booking_note: Emergency use only. Free, always open.

name: Bivacco Passeier
description: Larger emergency shelter maintained by CAI Passeier section on the high Passeier ridge route. 10 emergency bunks — one of the larger bivacchi in the Merano area. Used by ridge traversers and alpinists crossing between Passeier Valley and the Ötztal Alps.
access_info: Trail 211 from St. Leonhard in Passeier (4 hours, strenuous with significant elevation gain). Trail 212 from Pfelders (3 hours). Exposed high ridge — only attempt in stable weather.
josephine_note: Well-maintained and larger than most bivacchi — 10 bunks gives you a reasonable chance of space. Water from the snowfield below (melt first). The logbook has entries from Ötztal crossings going back to the 1980s.
highlights: Largest bivacco in the Merano area — 10 bunks
Crossroads of Passeier–Ötztal traverse routes
Well-maintained by CAI Passeier
booking_note: Free, always open. First-come. Bring all supplies — no facilities.

name: Bivacco Tiroler Kreuz
description: Small emergency shelter at the Tiroler Kreuz (Tyrolean Cross) saddle above Tirolo village, one of the most visited viewpoints near Merano. The bivouac is rarely needed by day hikers but serves as emergency refuge for those caught out on the high routes above Hochmuth.
access_info: Trail 27 from Tirolo village continues past Rifugio Hochmuth to the saddle (2 hours total from village). Trail 28 from Merano Obermais (2.5 hours). Well-marked, moderate difficulty.
josephine_note: If you're descending from the higher routes and the weather closes in, this shelter is your lifeline. On a clear day the saddle itself is a stunning lunch spot — the cross marks the spot where Tyrolean hunters rested for centuries.
highlights: Historic Tyrolean Cross saddle viewpoint
Emergency shelter above the Hochmuth Trail
Year-round access — useful in shoulder season
booking_note: Always open, free. Emergency use preferred but all welcome if caught out.
